response:
The error you are encountering stems from a fundamental mismatch between your .NET 7 cross-platform project and the WPF library, which relies on the older .NET Framework runtime on Windows. When you attempt to load the WPF assembly on Windows, the runtime expects the full .NET Framework 4.0 environment, but your application is running on the modern .NET 7 runtime which does not automatically include or reference the legacy PresentationFramework assembly in that specific version context.

To resolve this without downgrading your main application to .NET Framework, you must ensure that your WPF project is explicitly configured to target .NET 7 with the WPF workload installed, rather than targeting the legacy framework. This involves adding the appropriate SDK reference to your WPF project file so that the build system generates the correct assembly bindings for the modern runtime, effectively telling the compiler to use the .NET 7 compatible version of the WPF libraries.

cousin to the solution is the necessity of ensuring that the Windows deployment environment has the .NET 7 WPF workload installed, as the runtime libraries are not always present by default on a clean Windows installation. You should verify that the `Microsoft.NET.Sdk.WindowsDesktop` SDK is included in your project configuration and that the `UseWPF` property is set to true, which forces the build to include the necessary dependencies for the modern WPF stack.

Another critical step is to check your assembly binding redirects or, more likely in .NET 7, ensure that you are not manually referencing the old 4.0.0.0 version of PresentationFramework in your project files or code. The .NET 7 runtime expects a different version number for these assemblies, and any hardcoded references to the legacy version will trigger the file not found exception, so removing those explicit version constraints allows the runtime to resolve the correct assembly automatically.

Finally, after adjusting your project configurations and ensuring the correct workload is installed, you should clean and rebuild your entire solution to guarantee that all transitive dependencies are resolved correctly for the Windows platform. This approach allows you to maintain your cross-platform architecture using .NET 7 while successfully running the WPF UI on Windows and falling back to Cocoa on macOS without ever needing to revert to the .NET Framework.
